one_line_takeaway: Sharing DNA foundation-model embeddings is not privacy-preserving: per-token embeddings are essentially equivalent to sharing raw sequences, and even mean-pooled embeddings of short fragments can be reconstructed at >=90% similarity, with BPE tokenisation (DNABERT-2) offering the only meaningful, still-incomplete resistance.
key_finding: Per-token embeddings offer virtually no privacy: across all three models the MLP inverter reached Levenshtein similarity >99% and nucleotide accuracy >98%; NTv2 reconstructed ~99% of sequences with zero errors, and Evo 2 hit 99.8% accuracy with 79.5% exact matches at l=100 (DNABERT-2 and Evo 2 ~80% exact). Mean pooling provides only partial, insufficient protection: reconstruction degrades as length grows but stays well above baselines. Evo 2 (short seqs) and NTv2 are most vulnerable (NTv2 10-nt and Evo 2 15-25-nt fragments reconstructed at >=90% Levenshtein similarity), while DNABERT-2's variable-length BPE tokenisation is the most resilient (Levenshtein ~0.46-0.47, near the NN baseline). The correlation between embedding-distance and sequence-similarity is a strong predictor of attack success and can serve as a lightweight privacy-risk diagnostic.
core_contribution: An empirical privacy benchmark showing that shared embeddings from DNA foundation models are vulnerable to training-based model inversion attacks in an Embeddings-as-a-Service (EaaS) setting. The authors' own contribution is to quantify, across three genomic foundation models (DNABERT-2, Evo 2 7B, and Nucleotide Transformer v2) and two embedding-sharing strategies (per-token vs. mean-pooled), how faithfully an adversary can reconstruct the original nucleotide sequence from intercepted embeddings, and to identify tokenisation strategy and embedding-sequence-similarity correlation as the key determinants of attack success. Note: the model-inversion framing itself (Fredrikson et al. 2015) and the foundation models attacked are prior work; the novelty is the systematic privacy evaluation on genomic embeddings.
threat_model: An honest-but-collaborative EaaS scenario: data owner I1 holds labelled DNA sequences x_i in {A,C,G,T}^l, converts them to embeddings e_i = F(x_i) via a DNA foundation model F, and shares the embedding dataset with institution I2 for downstream training, believing the transformation hides the raw sequences. An adversary A intercepts the shared embeddings and trains an inversion model M: R^d -> {A,C,G,T}^l to reconstruct the original sequences. The attack is training-based (the adversary learns a decoder that minimises reconstruction loss), not optimisation-based. Identifying which foundation model produced an embedding is assumed trivial (distinct dimensions/distributions), so a separate inversion model is trained per (foundation model, sequence length) pair. The stakes are high because genomic data is immutable and uniquely identifying.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: (1) training used a single reference genome (hg38); although 1000 Genomes validation is consistent, the same reference was reused and a more sophisticated individual-level evaluation is possible. (2) Only basic reconstruction methods were used to show feasibility; more advanced approaches (recursive reconstruction, training-free methods) exist and could reconstruct more. (3) No defences (differential privacy, embedding perturbation) were evaluated. Additional caveats surfaced by the paper: the tokenisation-vs-resilience conclusion is confounded because DNABERT-2 also differs in model size (117M vs 500M/7B) and architecture, so tokenisation's protective effect cannot be cleanly disentangled; the paper is explicitly framed as an 'explorative benchmark' and often uses 'suspect/hypothesise' language for the tokenisation mechanism.

datasets_used: Human reference genome GRCh38/hg38 (primary; chr1-22, chrX, chrY, chrM), 100,000 unique non-overlapping N-free subsequences per configuration, split 70k/15k/15k
1000 Genomes Project (validation on real patient-level data; subsequences drawn equally from intronic and exonic regions, Appendix H)
quantitative_results: Per-token reconstruction (all 3 models): Levenshtein similarity >99%, nucleotide accuracy >98%
NTv2 per-token: ~99% of sequences reconstructed with zero errors
Evo 2 and DNABERT-2 per-token: ~80% of sequences reconstructed with zero errors
Evo 2 per-token at l=100: 99.8% nucleotide accuracy, 79.5% exact-match rate
Mean-pooled DNABERT-2 (most resilient): Levenshtein similarity 0.46 (l=10) to 0.47 (l=100), near NN baseline
Mean-pooled Evo 2 (non-monotonic): 0.58 Levenshtein at l=10, 0.98-0.99 at l=15-20
Mean-pooled NTv2 encoder inverter: Levenshtein 0.90 +/- 0.11 at l=10, 0.57 +/- 0.06 at l=100
NTv2 10-nt and Evo 2 15-25-nt mean-pooled fragments: >=90% Levenshtein similarity
Random baseline: ~25% nucleotide accuracy, 34-44% Levenshtein similarity
Spearman embedding-sequence correlation: Evo 2 peak 0.435 at l=20; NTv2 up to 0.231 at l=100; DNABERT-2 uniformly <=0.13
Embedding dimensions: DNABERT-2 768, NTv2 1024, Evo 2 4096; unique tokens observed: DNABERT-2 3,874 BPE, NTv2 3,897, Evo 2 4
Theoretical search space for l=100 is 4^100 ~ 1.6e60
baselines_compared: Inversion architectures compared against each other: Encoder-only Transformer (best), Decoder-only Transformer, 1D ResNet; small MLP for per-token setting
